questions.q: Which statement is true in any field $F$?
choices: Every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse.
questions.hint: A field requires that $F^{\times}=F\setminus\{0\}$ is a group under multiplication, so each nonzero element is invertible.
questions.explain: A field requires that $F^{\times}=F\setminus\{0\}$ is a group under multiplication, so each nonzero element is invertible.

questions.q: In the additive group $(\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z},+)$, what is the order of the element $4$?
questions.hint: The order is $12/\gcd(12,4)=3$, since $3\cdot4\equiv0\pmod{12}$.
questions.explain: The order is $12/\gcd(12,4)=3$, since $3\cdot4\equiv0\pmod{12}$.

questions.q: Which structure models symmetry most directly?
questions.hint: Symmetries compose and invert, exactly the group axioms.
questions.explain: Symmetries compose and invert, exactly the group axioms.

hard.q: Every field is a ring, but not every ring is a field. Which of the following rings is also a field?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}/7\mathbb{Z}$ is a field because $7$ is prime, so every nonzero element is invertible. $\mathbb{Z}$ lacks inverses for integers other than $\pm1$; $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ has zero-divisors; $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ is not a field since e.g. $x$ is not invertible.

hard.q: Let $G$ be a group of order $35$. By Lagrange's theorem, every element's order divides $|G|=35$. If no element has order $35$, the possible nontrivial orders are $5$ and $7$. How many elements of order $5$ must exist if there is exactly one subgroup of order $7$?
hard.hint: Count which elements are used up by the order-$7$ subgroup; the rest must have order $5$.
hard.explain: The unique subgroup of order $7$ contributes $6$ elements of order $7$ plus the identity. The remaining $35-7=28$ non-identity elements all have order $5$ (since their order must divide $35$ and cannot be $7$ or $35$). So there are $28$ elements of order $5$.

hard.q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a subring of a field need not itself be a field?
choices: $\mathbb{Q}\subseteq\mathbb{R}$; $\mathbb{Q}$ is a field, so this is not a counterexample
$\mathbb{Z}\subseteq\mathbb{Q}$; $\mathbb{Z}$ is a subring of the field $\mathbb{Q}$, but $\mathbb{Z}$ is not a field
$\mathbb{R}\subseteq\mathbb{C}$; $\mathbb{R}$ is a field, so this is not a counterexample
$\mathbb{F}_2\subseteq\mathbb{F}_4$; both are fields, so this is not a counterexample
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a subring of the field $\mathbb{Q}$ but fails to be a field because, for instance, $2\in\mathbb{Z}$ has no multiplicative inverse in $\mathbb{Z}$. The other options either involve two fields or are not counterexamples.

hard.q: Select all of the following groups that are abelian.
hard.hint: Additive groups of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ and their unit groups are always abelian; nonabelian examples usually involve permutations or matrices.
hard.explain: $(\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z},+)$ and $(\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z})^{\times}=\{1,3\}$ are both abelian (additive/cyclic and order-$2$ respectively). $S_3$ is the smallest nonabelian group (e.g. $(12)(13)\neq(13)(12)$); $D_4$ contains a rotation and a reflection that don't commute; and matrix multiplication in $GL_2(\mathbb{R})$ is famously noncommutative.

questions.q: In a Cayley table for a finite group $G$, each row is a permutation of $G$ primarily because of:
choices: Left cancellation (equivalently, left multiplication is bijective)
questions.hint: For fixed $g$, the map $h\mapsto gh$ is bijective with inverse $h\mapsto g^{-1}h$, so each row lists each element once.
questions.explain: For fixed $g$, the map $h\mapsto gh$ is bijective with inverse $h\mapsto g^{-1}h$, so each row lists each element once.

questions.q: If $|G|=24$ and $|H|=6$, how many left cosets does $H$ have in $G$?
questions.hint: By Lagrange, the index is $[G:H]=|G|/|H|=24/6=4$.
questions.explain: By Lagrange, the index is $[G:H]=|G|/|H|=24/6=4$.

questions.q: A subgroup $H\le G$ is normal exactly when:
questions.hint: Normality means left and right cosets coincide for every group element.
questions.explain: Normality means left and right cosets coincide for every group element.

hard.q: The dihedral group $D_4=\langle r,s\mid r^4=s^2=e,\,srs=r^{-1}\rangle$ has order $8$. Select all of the listed proper subgroups that are normal in $D_4$.
hard.hint: Index-$2$ subgroups are always normal; the center is always normal; a subgroup of order $2$ generated by one reflection has index $4$, so check conjugation by $r$.
hard.explain: $\{e,r^2\}$ is the center, automatically normal. The three order-$4$ subgroups $\langle r\rangle$, $\{e,r^2,s,r^2s\}$, and $\{e,r^2,rs,r^3s\}$ all have index $2$, hence normal. The order-$2$ subgroup $\langle s\rangle$ has index $4$ and is not normal: $rsr^{-1}=r\cdot s\cdot r^3 = r^2 s\notin\langle s\rangle$. So exactly the first four are normal.

hard.q: Using the Cayley table for $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$, the element $3$ has an order. Meanwhile the number of subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$ equals the number of divisors of $8$. What is that count?
hard.explain: The divisors of $8$ are $1,2,4,8$, giving four subgroups: $\{0\}$, $\{0,4\}$, $\{0,2,4,6\}$, and $\mathbb{Z}/8\mathbb{Z}$ itself. Cyclic groups of order $n$ have exactly one subgroup per divisor of $n$.

hard.q: Let $G$ be a finite group of order $|G|=105$, and let $H,K\le G$ be subgroups with $|H|=15$ and $|K|=35$. What is $|H\cap K|$?
hard.hint: $H\cap K$ is a subgroup of both $H$ and $K$; use Lagrange twice, then rule out the trivial case via a counting argument in $G$.
hard.explain: By Lagrange applied inside $H$ and inside $K$, $|H\cap K|$ divides $\gcd(|H|,|K|)=\gcd(15,35)=5$, so $|H\cap K|\in\{1,5\}$. If $|H\cap K|=1$, then $|HK|=|H|\cdot|K|/|H\cap K|=15\cdot 35=525>105=|G|$, impossible. So $|H\cap K|=5$.

questions.q: Select every statement that is true for an arbitrary group homomorphism $\varphi\colon G\to H$.
questions.hint: Kernels are normal; images are subgroups; the First Isomorphism Theorem ties them together; Lagrange via the quotient gives the size identity. Injectivity is special — equivalent to trivial kernel.
questions.explain: (0) $\ker\varphi\trianglelefteq G$ (kernels of group homomorphisms are always normal). (1) $\operatorname{im}\varphi\le H$ (the image is a subgroup). (2) First Isomorphism Theorem: $G/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. (3) is FALSE in general — $\varphi$ injective iff $\ker\varphi$ trivial. (4) follows from Lagrange: $|G|=|\ker\varphi|\cdot[G:\ker\varphi]=|\ker\varphi|\cdot|\operatorname{im}\varphi|$.

questions.q: How many elements does $\mathbb{F}_{3^2}$ have?
questions.hint: A finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ has exactly $p^n$ elements.
questions.explain: A finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ has exactly $p^n$ elements.

questions.q: In characteristic $p$, the Frobenius map on $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ is:
questions.hint: The Frobenius endomorphism raises each element to the $p$th power.
questions.explain: The Frobenius endomorphism raises each element to the $p$th power.

hard.q: Let $\varphi:\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ be the quotient map. By the First Isomorphism Theorem, $\mathbb{Z}/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. Which statement about this isomorphism is correct?
choices: $\ker\varphi=(6)$ and $\operatorname{im}\varphi=\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, so $\mathbb{Z}/(6)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ — a tautology confirming the theorem
hard.explain: The kernel of the quotient map $n\mapsto\bar{n}$ is $6\mathbb{Z}=(6)$, and the map is surjective, so $\operatorname{im}\varphi=\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$. The First Isomorphism Theorem then gives $\mathbb{Z}/(6)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, matching the original codomain exactly.

hard.q: The Frobenius on $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ generates the Galois group $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}/\mathbb{F}_p)$. How many elements does this Galois group have when $p=2,\ n=4$?
hard.explain: $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{F}_{2^4}/\mathbb{F}_2)\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$, cyclic of order $n=4$. It is generated by the Frobenius $x\mapsto x^2$.

hard.q: Which map is a group homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R},+)$ to $(\mathbb{R}^{>0},\times)$ with kernel $\{0\}$?
hard.explain: $e^{x+y}=e^x e^y$, so $x\mapsto e^x$ is a homomorphism $(\mathbb{R},+)\to(\mathbb{R}^{>0},\times)$. Its kernel is $\{x:e^x=1\}=\{0\}$, confirming injectivity. The other options either map to the wrong codomain or fail the homomorphism property.

questions.q: A proper ideal $\mathfrak{m}\subsetneq R$ of a commutative ring with $1$ is maximal exactly when:
choices: $R/\mathfrak{m}$ is an integral domain (but not necessarily a field)
questions.hint: By the correspondence theorem, ideals of $R/\mathfrak{m}$ correspond to ideals of $R$ containing $\mathfrak{m}$.
questions.explain: By the correspondence theorem, ideals of $R/\mathfrak{m}$ correspond to ideals of $R$ containing $\mathfrak{m}$. Maximality means the only such ideals are $\mathfrak{m}$ and $R$, i.e. $R/\mathfrak{m}$ has only $(0)$ and itself as ideals — which is exactly the field condition.

questions.q: In $\mathbb{Z}$, how many maximal ideals contain the ideal $(30)$?
questions.hint: Maximal ideals of $\mathbb{Z}$ are $(p)$ for $p$ prime.
questions.explain: Maximal ideals of $\mathbb{Z}$ are $(p)$ for $p$ prime. $(30)\subseteq(p)$ iff $p\mid 30$, so $p\in\{2,3,5\}$ — three of them.

questions.q: Which ring is not an integral domain?
questions.hint: In $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, $\overline{2}\cdot\overline{3}=\overline{0}$ with neither factor zero, so $(6)$ is not prime.
questions.explain: In $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$, $\overline{2}\cdot\overline{3}=\overline{0}$ with neither factor zero, so $(6)$ is not prime. The other three are domains.

hard.q: In $\mathbb{Z}[x]$, the ideal $(2,x)$ is maximal. Which quotient ring witnesses this, and what does it equal?
hard.hint: Quotient by one generator at a time; the order doesn't matter.
hard.explain: Quotienting by $(x)$ first gives $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}$; then quotienting by the image of $(2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\cong\mathbb{F}_2$. Since $\mathbb{F}_2$ is a field, $(2,x)$ is maximal. This chains ideal/quotient ring reasoning with the maximal $\Leftrightarrow$ field criterion.

hard.q: Let $R=\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)\cong\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (Gaussian integers). How many units does $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ have? (A unit $u$ satisfies $u\cdot v=1$ for some $v\in\mathbb{Z}[i]$.)
hard.explain: The norm $N(a+bi)=a^2+b^2$ is multiplicative. $u$ is a unit iff $N(u)=1$, i.e. $a^2+b^2=1$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. Solutions: $(\pm1,0),(0,\pm1)$, giving units $\{1,-1,i,-i\}$ — exactly $4$.

hard.q: A prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}$ in a commutative ring $R$ satisfies: $ab\in\mathfrak{p}\Rightarrow a\in\mathfrak{p}$ or $b\in\mathfrak{p}$. Which is a correct counterexample showing a prime ideal need not be maximal?
choices: $(0)$ in $\mathbb{Z}$: $\mathbb{Z}/(0)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is an integral domain but not a field, so $(0)$ is prime but not maximal
$(2)$ in $\mathbb{Z}$: $\mathbb{Z}/(2)\cong\mathbb{F}_2$ is a field, so $(2)$ is both prime and maximal
$(x)$ in $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2)$: $(x)$ is prime in this ring
Every prime ideal is maximal in any Noetherian ring
hard.explain: $(0)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ is prime because $\mathbb{Z}$ is a domain ($ab=0\Rightarrow a=0$ or $b=0$), but $(0)$ is not maximal since it is properly contained in $(2),(3),(5),\ldots$ This shows prime $\not\Rightarrow$ maximal in general. Option B gives a maximal-prime example (not a counterexample). Option C is wrong: $(x)$ in $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2)$ is not prime since $x\cdot x=x^2=0\in(x)$ but $x\notin(0)$.

questions.q: What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]$?
questions.hint: $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$ (minimal poly $x^2-2$), and $\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, so $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})]=2$.
questions.explain: $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$ (minimal poly $x^2-2$), and $\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, so $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})]=2$. The Tower Law gives $2\cdot 2=4$.

questions.q: The minimal polynomial $m_\alpha\in F[x]$ of an algebraic element $\alpha$ is characterized by:
choices: the unique monic irreducible $p\in F[x]$ with $p(\alpha)=0$
any monic polynomial with $\alpha$ as a root
the polynomial of largest degree vanishing on $\alpha$
the product of all polynomials vanishing on $\alpha$
questions.hint: The ideal $\{p\in F[x]:p(\alpha)=0\}$ is principal in the PID $F[x]$, generated by a unique monic irreducible — that generator is the minimal polynomial.
questions.explain: The ideal $\{p\in F[x]:p(\alpha)=0\}$ is principal in the PID $F[x]$, generated by a unique monic irreducible — that generator is the minimal polynomial.

questions.q: Let $\alpha=\sqrt[3]{2}$. What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]$?
questions.hint: The minimal polynomial of $\sqrt[3]{2}$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ is $x^3-2$, irreducible by Eisenstein at $p=2$.
questions.explain: The minimal polynomial of $\sqrt[3]{2}$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ is $x^3-2$, irreducible by Eisenstein at $p=2$. Its degree $3$ is the extension degree.

hard.q: Let $\alpha$ be a root of the irreducible $p(x)=x^4-2x^2-2\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$ (irreducible by Eisenstein shifted: $p(x+1)=x^4+4x^3+4x^2-4$ — or confirm by rational-root test plus irreducibility mod $3$). What is $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]$?
hard.explain: Since $p(x)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$ and $\alpha$ is a root, $\mathbb{Q}(\alpha)\cong\mathbb{Q}[x]/(p(x))$ and $[\mathbb{Q}(\alpha):\mathbb{Q}]=\deg p=4$. The Tower Law is not needed here — the extension degree equals the degree of the minimal polynomial.

hard.q: The Tower Law says $[L:F]=[L:K][K:F]$ for $F\subseteq K\subseteq L$. Which chain gives $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt[3]{5}):\mathbb{Q}]=6$?
choices: $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt[3]{5}):\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})]\cdot[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=3\cdot 2=6$, because $\sqrt[3]{5}\notin\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$ so its minimal polynomial over that field still has degree $3$
The two extensions interfere, forcing degree $12$
Degree is $5$ because $2$ and $5$ are distinct primes
hard.hint: A degree-3 polynomial stays irreducible over a degree-2 extension when $\gcd(2,3)=1$.
hard.explain: $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$. Since $\sqrt[3]{5}$ has minimal polynomial $x^3-5$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ and $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}):\mathbb{Q}]=2$ is coprime to $3$, the polynomial $x^3-5$ remains irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, giving degree-$3$ second step. Tower Law: $2\times 3=6$.

hard.q: The finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p^n}$ can be viewed as a field extension of $\mathbb{F}_p$ of degree $n$. Combining this with the minimal-polynomial characterization: what is the minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_2$ of a primitive element $\alpha\in\mathbb{F}_{2^3}$ (i.e., an element generating the multiplicative group of order $7$)?
choices: A degree-$3$ irreducible polynomial over $\mathbb{F}_2$, e.g. $x^3+x+1$
hard.explain: $[\mathbb{F}_{2^3}:\mathbb{F}_2]=3$, so the minimal polynomial of any primitive element must have degree exactly $3$. The polynomial $x^3+x+1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$ (no roots in $\mathbb{F}_2$) and serves as a minimal polynomial for a generator. Degree-$7$ would make no sense as a minimal poly; degree $2$ gives $\mathbb{F}_4\neq\mathbb{F}_8$.

questions.q: If $|G|=60$ and $H\le G$ with $|H|=12$, how many left cosets $gH$ does $H$ have in $G$?
questions.hint: By Lagrange, $[G:H]=|G|/|H|=60/12=5$.
questions.explain: By Lagrange, $[G:H]=|G|/|H|=60/12=5$.

questions.q: Let $H\le G$ and $g\in G$. Which statement about the left coset $gH$ is always true?
choices: $|gH|=|H|$ because $h\mapsto gh$ is a bijection $H\to gH$
questions.hint: Left multiplication by $g$ is a bijection of $G$, so it sends $H$ to a set of the same cardinality.
questions.explain: Left multiplication by $g$ is a bijection of $G$, so it sends $H$ to a set of the same cardinality. Cosets are not subgroups unless $g\in H$ (then $gH=H$ contains $e$); left and right cosets coincide only when $H$ is normal.

questions.q: Select all statements equivalent to $H\trianglelefteq G$ (i.e., $H$ is normal in $G$).
choices: $H$ is the kernel of some homomorphism $\varphi: G\to K$
questions.hint: Normality is intrinsic to the $H\hookrightarrow G$ embedding; it doesn't depend on internal structure of $H$.
questions.explain: Coset equality $gH=Hg$, closure under conjugation $gHg^{-1}\subseteq H$, and being the kernel of a homomorphism are the three standard equivalent definitions of normality. Prime index and abelianness can occur with non-normal or with normal subgroups; neither implies normality in general (though index $2$ always does).

hard.q: Let $H,K\le G$ be finite subgroups with $\gcd(|H|,|K|)=1$. What is $|H\cap K|$?
choices: $|H\cap K|=\gcd(|H|,|K|)=1$ vacuously, so it could be any divisor of $1$ — namely $1$
$|H\cap K|$ depends on $G$ and cannot be determined
hard.hint: $H\cap K$ is a subgroup of both $H$ and $K$; apply Lagrange twice.
hard.explain: Because $H\cap K\le H$, $|H\cap K|$ divides $|H|$; similarly it divides $|K|$. So $|H\cap K|\mid\gcd(|H|,|K|)=1$, forcing $|H\cap K|=1$.

hard.q: Consider $H=\langle (12)\rangle\le S_3$, of order $2$. Is $H$ normal in $S_3$?
choices: No: conjugating $(12)$ by $(13)$ gives $(23)\notin H$, so $gHg^{-1}\ne H$
hard.hint: Normal subgroups must be closed under conjugation by every group element.
hard.explain: $(13)(12)(13)^{-1}=(13)(12)(13)=(23)$, which lies outside $H=\{e,(12)\}$. Hence $H$ is not normal. Only index-$2$ subgroups are automatically normal; prime index alone is not enough when the index is $>2$.

hard.q: Let $G$ be a finite group and suppose $H\le G$ with $[G:H]=3$. The left cosets partition $G$ into $3$ blocks each of size $|H|$. If additionally $|G|=12$, what is $|H|$?

questions.q: When is the coset multiplication $(aN)(bN)=(ab)N$ well-defined on $G/N$?
choices: Exactly when $N$ is a normal subgroup of $G$
Only when $N$ is the trivial subgroup
questions.hint: The product of cosets is independent of representatives iff $aN=Na$ for all $a$, which is the defining property of a normal subgroup.
questions.explain: The product of cosets is independent of representatives iff $aN=Na$ for all $a$, which is the defining property of a normal subgroup.

questions.q: What is $|G/N|$ when $|G|=24$ and $|N|=6$ (with $N\trianglelefteq G$)?
The first isomorphism theorem says that for a surjective homomorphism $\varphi:G\twoheadrightarrow H$,
questions.hint: Quotienting by the kernel identifies exactly those pairs that $\varphi$ sends to the same element, so $G/\ker\varphi\to\mathrm{im}\,\varphi=H$ is an isomorphism.
questions.explain: Quotienting by the kernel identifies exactly those pairs that $\varphi$ sends to the same element, so $G/\ker\varphi\to\mathrm{im}\,\varphi=H$ is an isomorphism.

hard.q: Let $H\le G$ and $N\trianglelefteq G$. The second isomorphism theorem gives an isomorphism involving the subgroup $HN$ and the quotient $H/(H\cap N)$. Which is it?
hard.hint: The inclusion $H\hookrightarrow HN$ composed with the projection $HN\twoheadrightarrow HN/N$ has kernel $H\cap N$.
hard.explain: Consider $\varphi:H\to HN/N$, $h\mapsto hN$. It is surjective (every coset of $N$ in $HN$ has an $H$-representative) and $\ker\varphi=H\cap N$. The first iso theorem gives $H/(H\cap N)\cong HN/N$.

hard.q: Using the third isomorphism theorem with $G=\mathbb{Z}$, $N=6\mathbb{Z}$, $M=2\mathbb{Z}\supseteq N$, we have $(G/N)/(M/N)\cong G/M$. What is $|(G/N)/(M/N)|$?
hard.hint: $G/M=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$.
hard.explain: $G/M=\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}$, of order $2$. The third isomorphism theorem matches $|(G/N)/(M/N)|=|G/M|=2$.

hard.q: Why is the alternating group $A_n$ (for $n\ge 5$) a witness to failure of the converse of Lagrange for quotients?
choices: $A_n$ is simple for $n\ge 5$, so it has no nontrivial normal subgroups — hence no nontrivial quotient groups, even though it has plenty of subgroups with proper divisor orders
hard.explain: For $n\ge 5$, $A_n$ is simple, so its only normal subgroups are $\{e\}$ and $A_n$ itself. It still contains subgroups of many orders dividing $|A_n|$, but none of them are normal, so none can be used to form a nontrivial proper quotient.

questions.q: Let $G=S_4$ act on the set $X=\{1,2,3,4\}$ by permutation. The orbit of $1\in X$ has size $4$, so by orbit-stabilizer, $|\mathrm{Stab}(1)|=|G|/4=?$
questions.explain: $|G|/|\mathrm{orbit}(1)|=24/4=6$. The stabilizer of $1$ is the subgroup of permutations fixing $1$, isomorphic to $S_3$ on $\{2,3,4\}$ — of order $6$.

questions.q: Arrange the steps in the standard derivation of Burnside's lemma: $\#\text{orbits} = \tfrac{1}{|G|}\sum_{g\in G}|\mathrm{Fix}(g)|$.
items: Apply orbit-stabilizer: $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = |G|/|\mathrm{orbit}(x)|$, so $\sum_{x\in X}|\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = \sum_{\text{orbits}\,\mathcal O}\sum_{x\in\mathcal O}|G|/|\mathcal O| = |G|\cdot\#\text{orbits}$.
Count the size of $S = \{(g,x)\in G\times X : g\cdot x = x\}$ in two ways.
Combine the two expressions: $\sum_{g\in G}|\mathrm{Fix}(g)| = |G|\cdot\#\text{orbits}$, divide by $|G|$.
questions.hint: Define the incidence set, count two ways, apply orbit-stabilizer, divide by $|G|$.
questions.explain: Burnside's lemma is the classic two-way counting argument. (1) Set $S = \{(g,x): g\cdot x = x\}$. (2) Counting by $g$: $|S| = \sum_g |\mathrm{Fix}(g)|$. (3) Counting by $x$: $|S| = \sum_x |\mathrm{Stab}(x)|$. (4) By orbit-stabilizer, $\sum_x |\mathrm{Stab}(x)| = \sum_{\mathcal O}\sum_{x\in\mathcal O}|G|/|\mathcal O| = \sum_{\mathcal O}|G| = |G|\cdot\#\text{orbits}$. (5) Equating and dividing by $|G|$ gives the result — orbits = average fixed-point count.

questions.q: Apply Burnside to $G=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$ acting on $2$-colorings of a $3$-bead necklace by rotation. There are $2^3=8$ colorings. The identity fixes all $8$; each nontrivial rotation fixes $2$ (constant colorings). The orbit count is $(8+2+2)/3=?$
questions.explain: $(8+2+2)/3=12/3=4$. The four orbits correspond to distinct necklaces up to rotation.

hard.q: Count the distinct $2$-colorings of the $4$ vertices of a square under the full dihedral group $D_4$ ($|D_4|=8$). Fixed counts: $e{:}16$, $r{:}2$, $r^2{:}4$, $r^3{:}2$, two diagonal reflections each fix $2^3=8$, two edge-midpoint reflections each fix $2^2=4$. Apply Burnside:
hard.hint: Sum the fixed counts, then divide by $|D_4|=8$.
hard.explain: Sum: $16+2+4+2+8+8+4+4=48$. By Burnside, the number of orbits is $48/8=6$.

hard.q: Why can Burnside's lemma fail for an infinite group $G$ acting on a finite set $X$?
choices: The sum $\sum_{g\in G}|\mathrm{Fix}(g)|$ is infinite, and $|G|$ is infinite, so the ratio is undefined
Infinite groups cannot act on finite sets
Orbits of infinite groups are always infinite
hard.hint: Burnside is an averaging argument; averaging over an infinite group needs the group to be (at least) locally finite or measure-preserving.
hard.explain: Burnside's lemma relies on averaging fixed-point counts over a finite group. For infinite $G$, both numerator and denominator can be infinite. Infinite groups certainly can act on finite sets — e.g. $\mathbb{Z}$ acts on $\{0,1\}$ via $n\mapsto n\bmod 2$.

hard.q: By orbit-stabilizer, if $G$ acts on $X$ and $|G|=30$, $|\mathrm{orbit}(x)|=10$, what is $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|$?
hard.explain: $|\mathrm{Stab}(x)|=|G|/|\mathrm{orbit}(x)|=30/10=3$.

questions.q: We are proving every group of order $15$ is cyclic. Pick the correct next step.
steps: Let $|G|=15=3\cdot 5$. By Sylow III, the number $n_3$ of Sylow $3$-subgroups satisfies $n_3\mid 5$ and $n_3\equiv 1\pmod 3$.
Divisors of $5$ are $\{1,5\}$; only $1\equiv 1\pmod 3$, so $n_3=1$. Similarly $n_5\mid 3$ and $n_5\equiv 1\pmod 5$ force $n_5=1$.

Hence $G$ has a unique (therefore normal) Sylow $3$-subgroup $P_3\cong\mathbb Z/3$ and a unique normal Sylow $5$-subgroup $P_5\cong\mathbb Z/5$. Since $\gcd(3,5)=1$, $P_3\cap P_5=\{e\}$, and $|P_3 P_5|=15=|G|$, so $G=P_3 P_5$.
choices: Two normal subgroups of coprime orders with trivial intersection and full product give a direct product, so $G\cong P_3\times P_5\cong\mathbb Z/3\times\mathbb Z/5\cong\mathbb Z/15$ — cyclic.
Conclude $G\cong S_3$ since $|S_3|=6$ has Sylow structure compatible with order $15$.
Conclude $G\cong P_3\rtimes P_5$ for some nontrivial action.
Conclude $G$ is non-abelian since both Sylow subgroups are normal.
questions.hint: Two normal subgroups of coprime orders with trivial intersection and full product give an internal direct product.
questions.explain: When $N,M\trianglelefteq G$ satisfy $N\cap M=\{e\}$ and $NM=G$, we have $G\cong N\times M$. Here $G\cong\mathbb Z/3\times\mathbb Z/5\cong\mathbb Z/15$ (Chinese Remainder), the unique group of order $15$ — necessarily abelian and cyclic.

questions.q: For $|G|=56=2^3\cdot 7$, what is the maximal possible order of a Sylow $2$-subgroup?
questions.hint: A Sylow $p$-subgroup of a group of order $p^k m$ with $\gcd(p,m)=1$ has order exactly $p^k$.
questions.explain: A Sylow $p$-subgroup of a group of order $p^k m$ with $\gcd(p,m)=1$ has order exactly $p^k$. Here $p=2$, $k=3$, so order $2^3=8$.

questions.q: Which of the following are true about Sylow $p$-subgroups of a finite group $G$ (with $|G|=p^k m$, $\gcd(p,m)=1$)?
choices: They all have the same order $p^k$
If $n_p=1$ the unique Sylow $p$-subgroup is normal in $G$
questions.hint: All five are part of the Sylow theorems.
questions.explain: All five are part of the Sylow theorems. Existence gives order $p^k$; Sylow II gives conjugacy; Sylow III gives the divisibility and congruence on $n_p$; a unique Sylow subgroup is necessarily closed under conjugation, hence normal.

hard.q: Let $|G|=30=2\cdot 3\cdot 5$. Which argument shows that $G$ cannot be simple?
choices: A counting argument on elements of order $3$ and $5$ shows at least one of $n_3$ or $n_5$ must equal $1$
Every group of order $30$ is abelian
$30$ is squarefree, so all Sylow subgroups commute
hard.hint: If $n_3=10$ and $n_5=6$, count the elements of order $3$ and $5$: there would be more than $30$ total.
hard.explain: $n_3\in\{1,10\}$ and $n_5\in\{1,6\}$. If $n_3=10$ and $n_5=6$, that's $10\cdot 2=20$ elements of order $3$ plus $6\cdot 4=24$ elements of order $5$, for $44>30$ elements — contradiction. So at least one Sylow subgroup is unique, hence normal, and $G$ isn't simple.

hard.q: Let $p<q$ be primes with $p\nmid(q-1)$, and $|G|=pq$. Which conclusion is correct?
choices: $G$ is nonabelian and unique up to isomorphism
$n_p$ could be either $1$ or $q$, so $G$ may not be abelian
hard.hint: Show $n_p=1$ by combining Sylow's divisibility and congruence with the hypothesis $p\nmid(q-1)$.
hard.explain: $n_q\mid p$ and $n_q\equiv 1\pmod q$; since $p<q$, $n_q=1$. $n_p\mid q$ and $n_p\equiv 1\pmod p$; the divisors of $q$ are $1,q$, and $q\equiv 1\pmod p$ would force $p\mid(q-1)$, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence $n_p=1$. Both Sylows are normal and meet trivially, so $G\cong\mathbb{Z}/p\times\mathbb{Z}/q\cong\mathbb{Z}/pq$.

hard.q: For $|G|=56=2^3\cdot 7$, list the divisors of $8$ congruent to $1\pmod 7$; this tells you the possible values of $n_7$. The count of such divisors is:
hard.hint: Divisors of $8$: $1,2,4,8$. Which are $\equiv 1\pmod 7$?
hard.explain: Among $\{1,2,4,8\}$, the ones $\equiv 1\pmod 7$ are $1$ and $8$, so $n_7\in\{1,8\}$ — two possibilities. (A classical counting argument then forces $n_7=1$, but the raw Sylow constraint allows two.)

questions.q: How many abelian groups of order $8$ are there, up to isomorphism?
questions.hint: The partitions of $3$ (the $2$-adic valuation) are $3$, $2+1$, $1+1+1$, giving $\mathbb{Z}/8$, $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2$, and $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^3$ — three groups.
questions.explain: The partitions of $3$ (the $2$-adic valuation) are $3$, $2+1$, $1+1+1$, giving $\mathbb{Z}/8$, $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2$, and $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^3$ — three groups.

questions.q: How many abelian groups of order $12$ are there?
questions.explain: $12=2^2\cdot 3$. Partitions of $2$ (for $p=2$): $2$ or $1+1$; partitions of $1$ (for $p=3$): $1$. Product: $2\cdot 1=2$ groups — $\mathbb{Z}/12\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$ and $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$.

questions.q: Which is the invariant-factor form of $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/6$?
questions.hint: Decompose into prime-power parts: $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/6\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$.
questions.explain: Decompose into prime-power parts: $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/6\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$. Recombine as invariant factors $d_1\mid d_2$: pair smaller prime powers into $\mathbb{Z}/2$, and larger into $\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3\cong\mathbb{Z}/12$. So $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12$.

hard.q: How many abelian groups of order $72=2^3\cdot 3^2$ are there up to isomorphism?
hard.hint: Multiply the number of partitions of $3$ by the number of partitions of $2$.
hard.explain: Partitions of $3$: $3,\,2+1,\,1+1+1$ — three of them. Partitions of $2$: $2,\,1+1$ — two. Product: $3\cdot 2=6$.

hard.q: Which abelian group has invariant factors $(2,4,12)$ (i.e., is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12$)?
choices: An abelian group of order $96$ with elementary-divisor form $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$
It is not a valid invariant-factor tuple because $2\nmid 4$
hard.hint: Decompose each $\mathbb{Z}/d_i$ into prime-power cyclic factors and combine.
hard.explain: $\mathbb{Z}/12\cong\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$. So $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/12\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/4\oplus\mathbb{Z}/3$. The order is $2\cdot 4\cdot 12=96$. The invariant-factor tuple $(2,4,12)$ is valid because $2\mid 4\mid 12$.

hard.q: Why is the classification uniquely determined by the elementary divisors (i.e., the multiset of prime-power cyclic summands)?
choices: Every finitely generated abelian group has a unique decomposition into cyclic groups of prime-power order, up to reordering — this is the structure theorem for PIDs applied to $\mathbb{Z}$
The decomposition is unique because $\mathbb{Z}$ is finite
hard.explain: The structure theorem for finitely generated modules over a PID, specialized to $R=\mathbb{Z}$, gives a canonical decomposition; the multisets of elementary divisors (or equivalently invariant factors) are invariants of the isomorphism class.

questions.q: Apply Eisenstein's criterion at $p=3$ to $f(x)=x^3+3x+3\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$. Which conditions hold, showing irreducibility?
choices: $3\nmid 1$ (leading), $3\mid 3$ (lower coefficients), $9\nmid 3$ (constant term not divisible by $p^2$) — so $f$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$
questions.hint: Eisenstein needs: $p\nmid a_n$ (leading), $p\mid a_i$ for $i<n$, and $p^2\nmid a_0$.
questions.explain:
Eisenstein needs: $p
mid a_n$ (leading), $p\mid a_i$ for $i<n$, and $p^2
mid a_0$. With $p=3$: $3
mid 1$, $3\mid 0$ and $3\mid 3$, and $9
mid 3$. So $f$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$ (and over $\mathbb{Z}$ by Gauss's lemma).

questions.q: What are the rational-root candidates for $f(x)=2x^2+3x+1$ from the rational-root test?
questions.hint: Candidates are $\pm(\text{divisors of }a_0)/(\text{divisors of }a_n)=\pm(1)/\pm(1,2)=\pm 1, \pm\tfrac{1}{2}$.
questions.explain: Candidates are $\pm(\text{divisors of }a_0)/(\text{divisors of }a_n)=\pm(1)/\pm(1,2)=\pm 1, \pm\tfrac{1}{2}$. Testing: $f(-1)=0$ and $f(-\tfrac{1}{2})=0$, so $f=(2x+1)(x+1)$.

questions.q: If $f\in k[x]$ has degree $5$ and $g\in k[x]$ has degree $3$, what is $\deg(fg)$?
questions.hint: In a polynomial ring over a field (or any integral domain), $\deg(fg)=\deg f+\deg g=5+3=8$.
questions.explain: In a polynomial ring over a field (or any integral domain), $\deg(fg)=\deg f+\deg g=5+3=8$.

hard.q: Consider $f(x)=x^4+1\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$. Which of the following is correct?
choices: $f$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$ (no rational roots, no real quadratic factorization over $\mathbb{Q}$)
hard.hint: Use Eisenstein after the shift $x\mapsto x+1$.
hard.explain: $f(x+1)=(x+1)^4+1=x^4+4x^3+6x^2+4x+2$. Eisenstein at $p=2$: $2\nmid 1$, $2\mid 4,6,4,2$, $4\nmid 2$. So $f(x+1)$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$, hence so is $f(x)$. The factorization into $(x^2\pm\sqrt{2}x+1)$ exists only over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$, not $\mathbb{Q}$.

hard.q: Gauss's lemma states: if $f\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$ is primitive (gcd of coefficients is $1$) and factors as $f=gh$ in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$, then ...
choices: $f$ factors as $f=g'h'$ in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ with $g'$, $h'$ primitive and scalar multiples (rationally) of $g$, $h$ — i.e., a $\mathbb{Q}[x]$ factorization of a primitive integer polynomial lifts to $\mathbb{Z}[x]$
hard.hint: The point is that primitivity is stable under multiplication.
hard.explain: Gauss's lemma: the product of primitive polynomials is primitive, and consequently a factorization in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$ of a primitive integer polynomial can be rescaled to a factorization in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$. This is why irreducibility in $\mathbb{Q}[x]$ and in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$ agree for primitive polynomials.

hard.q: Reduce $f(x)=x^3+x+1$ mod $2$. How many roots does it have in $\mathbb{F}_2=\{0,1\}$?
hard.hint: Evaluate at $0$ and at $1$ in $\mathbb{F}_2$.
hard.explain: $f(0)=1$, $f(1)=1+1+1=1$ in $\mathbb{F}_2$. No roots, so $\bar{f}\in\mathbb{F}_2[x]$ has no linear factors, and since it's degree $3$, it is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$. This lifts to show $f$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Z}$ (and $\mathbb{Q}$) too.

semidirect-products-ses.title: Semidirect products and short exact sequences

questions.q: For a semidirect product $G=N\rtimes_\varphi H$ with $|N|=8$ and $|H|=3$, what is $|G|$?
questions.hint: $|G|=|N|\cdot|H|=8\cdot 3=24$, independent of $\varphi$.
questions.explain: $|G|=|N|\cdot|H|=8\cdot 3=24$, independent of $\varphi$.

questions.q: Which group is $\mathbb{Z}/n\rtimes_\varphi\mathbb{Z}/2$ when $\varphi$ sends the generator of $\mathbb{Z}/2$ to the inversion automorphism $a\mapsto -a$ of $\mathbb{Z}/n$?
questions.hint: By construction $D_n=\langle r,s\mid r^n=s^2=e,\,srs=r^{-1}\rangle$; with $N=\langle r\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/n$ and $H=\langle s\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$ and $\varphi(s)=$ inversion, the twisted…
questions.explain: By construction $D_n=\langle r,s\mid r^n=s^2=e,\,srs=r^{-1}\rangle$; with $N=\langle r\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/n$ and $H=\langle s\rangle\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$ and $\varphi(s)=$ inversion, the twisted product reproduces the dihedral relations exactly.

questions.q: A short exact sequence $1\to N\to G\to H\to 1$ splits when
choices: There is a homomorphism $s:H\to G$ with $\pi\circ s=\mathrm{id}_H$, where $\pi:G\twoheadrightarrow H$ is the quotient map
questions.hint: Splitting means the quotient map has a homomorphism section.
questions.explain: Splitting means the quotient map has a homomorphism section. Equivalently, $G$ is isomorphic to a semidirect product $N\rtimes H$.

hard.q: Let $|G|=2p$ with $p$ an odd prime. Classify $G$ up to isomorphism.
choices: $G\cong\mathbb{Z}/(2p)$ or $G\cong D_p$; the choice depends on whether the semidirect-product twist is trivial or the inversion automorphism
hard.hint: Sylow gives a unique normal Sylow $p$-subgroup $N$; then $G=N\rtimes\mathbb{Z}/2$, and $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/p)\cong\mathbb{Z}/(p-1)$ has a unique order-$2$ element (inversion).
hard.explain: $n_p\mid 2$ and $n_p\equiv 1\pmod p$, so $n_p=1$ (since $p>2$ implies $2\not\equiv 1\pmod p$). So $N$ is normal; complement $\mathbb{Z}/2$ exists (Schur-Zassenhaus or Sylow argument). The only homomorphisms $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/p)\cong\mathbb{Z}/(p-1)$ send the generator to either the identity (giving $\mathbb{Z}/p\times\mathbb{Z}/2=\mathbb{Z}/(2p)$) or to inversion (giving $D_p$).

hard.q: When is an internal semidirect product $G=N\rtimes H$ actually a direct product $N\times H$?
choices: Exactly when the action $\varphi:H\to\mathrm{Aut}(N)$ is trivial, i.e. every element of $H$ commutes with every element of $N$
hard.hint: The multiplication in $N\rtimes_\varphi H$ reduces to that of $N\times H$ precisely when $\varphi$ is trivial.
hard.explain: By definition $(n_1,h_1)(n_2,h_2)=(n_1\varphi_{h_1}(n_2),h_1 h_2)$; this collapses to componentwise multiplication iff $\varphi_{h}$ is the identity for all $h$, i.e. $\varphi$ is trivial. Coprimality and abelianness are red herrings.

hard.q: How many nonisomorphic semidirect products $\mathbb{Z}/7\rtimes_\varphi\mathbb{Z}/3$ are there (counting both trivial and nontrivial $\varphi$)?
hard.hint: $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/7)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6$. How many subgroups of order dividing $3$?
hard.explain: Homomorphisms $\mathbb{Z}/3\to\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{Z}/7)\cong\mathbb{Z}/6$ correspond to elements of order dividing $3$ in $\mathbb{Z}/6$, which form the unique subgroup of order $3$. So there are $3$ homomorphisms, but two of the nontrivial ones give isomorphic semidirect products (they differ by an automorphism of $\mathbb{Z}/3$). Thus there are $2$ isomorphism classes: the trivial one ($\mathbb{Z}/21$) and the nonabelian one (order $21$).
